skill.name: database-operations
skill.description: Guidance for writing efficient SQLAlchemy database queries with repository classes, bulk operations, and joins.
skill.summary: Write efficient SQLAlchemy queries with repository-based patterns.
skill.author: Crossbill-Highlights

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed security issue in the single SKILL.md file. The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at line 3 and system reconnaissance alert at line 6 are false positives from benign SQLAlchemy guidance.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The flagged text on line 3 is the skill description and only mentions SQLAlchemy database query guidance. No cryptographic algorithm, credential handling, or hashing behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain metadata describing SQLAlchemy use. There is no code or instruction involving cryptography.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The flagged text on line 6 recommends avoiding for-loops and using SQLAlchemy bulk operations or SQL joins. It does not request host, user, process, network, or filesystem reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is database query efficiency guidance. No operating system commands or reconnaissance targets are present.

content.value_statement: Database code can become slow and hard to maintain when queries are scattered across the application. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward repository classes, bulk operations, and efficient joins.
actual_capabilities: Recommends SQLAlchemy bulk operations instead of repeated per-row loops.
Encourages SQL joins for loading related data in a single query.
Promotes repository classes as the boundary for database operations.
Guides review of database access patterns for maintainability.
Supports Python teams using SQLAlchemy as an ORM or query toolkit.
limitations: Does not provide schema-specific query implementations.
Does not replace database profiling or execution plan analysis.
Does not define transaction, migration, or connection management rules.
Does not cover non-SQLAlchemy database libraries.

use_cases.title: Refactor Slow ORM Loops
use_cases.description: Convert repeated row-by-row database work into bulk SQLAlchemy operations where the model supports it.

use_cases.title: Review Repository Boundaries
use_cases.description: Check whether database operations belong inside repository classes instead of service or route handlers.

use_cases.title: Improve Related Data Loading
use_cases.description: Plan SQL joins that fetch related data in fewer database round trips.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Query Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small database function
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SQLAlchemy database operation. Identify any for-loops that should become bulk operations or joins.

prompt_templates.title: Move Logic to a Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this service method and suggest how to move database operations into a repository class.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Database Round Trips
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this SQLAlchemy workflow and suggest joins or bulk operations that reduce repeated queries.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Repository Query Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repository-level SQLAlchemy query strategy for this feature. Prioritize bulk operations, joins, and maintainable boundaries.

output_examples.input: A handler loops through user records and saves each update separately.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the loop as a likely performance issue and recommends a repository method using a bulk update approach.

output_examples.input: A service fetches orders and then loads customer data one record at a time.
output_examples.output: The response recommends loading related customer data with a join inside a repository method.

output_examples.input: A route handler contains direct SQLAlchemy session operations.
output_examples.output: The response suggests moving database access into a repository class and keeping the route focused on request handling.

best_practices: Keep database operations inside repository classes.
Prefer SQLAlchemy bulk methods for repeated updates or inserts.
Use joins when related data can be fetched in one query.
anti_patterns: Do not perform database writes inside repeated application-level loops when a bulk operation fits.
Do not scatter direct SQLAlchemy session logic across handlers and services.
Do not load related data with repeated queries when a join is clear and maintainable.

faq.question: Does this skill write SQLAlchemy code?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for SQLAlchemy query structure. The final implementation still depends on your models and schema.

faq.question: Can it optimize any database query?
faq.answer: It focuses on common SQLAlchemy patterns. Complex tuning still needs profiling, indexes, and database-specific analysis.

faq.question: Does it require repository classes?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends repository classes as the main location for database operations.

faq.question: Does it support async SQLAlchemy?
faq.answer: The guidance is general. Async details must be adapted to your session and transaction model.

faq.question: Is this useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help reviewers find loops, repeated queries, and misplaced database logic.

faq.question: Does it access databases directly?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation only and contains no scripts, commands, or database credentials.
